How to Teach Children to Write Words Like They Sound A couple of weeks ago, I wrote a blog entry that detailed how the teachers at my school were supposed to use a new lesson plan format that included step by step directions to teach children new skills. Last week, I wrote another lesson that was similar in nature to that one, but was for teaching children to write words like they sound rather than for teaching children to sound out CVC words. This lesson is based on the "RISE/TESS" format that we are being expected to use in my school district these days. And while I REALLY dislike being told what to do, I can see the benefits of teaching children the steps to certain skills in this way. I have noticed that since I started trying out this type of lesson, some of my lower children have been catching on to some of the trickier skills more quickly than I would have expected, based on my previous experience. It is probably based on the use of the visual aid, combined with the motions that I add into it, and the fact that I am leaving the posters up on the wall for the skills that I know take a long time to master, such as sounding out CVC words and now writing words like they sound. That way, the children (and I) can refer to them when they get stuck. Here are the steps: Step 1. Say the word. Step 2. Stretch out it. Step 3. Write the first sound. Step 4. Stretch out it. Step 5. Write the middle sound. Step 6. Stretch out it. Step 7. Write the ending sound. Below are the most important elements of the lesson. You can also download the entire lesson plan with the steps HERE... and the pictures that go with it HERE. 1. Children are told the "Big Idea" of the lesson, (AKA the main idea of the lesson,) and are expected to be able to repeat it back. The Big Idea of this lesson is: Writing words the way they sound is saying the words out loud and writing down the sounds that you hear. 2. The teacher models writing one of the words, and then introduces the steps, and then models writing one more word. The RISE trainers refer to this as a "Model Sandwich." When I modeled the steps, I also taught them a motion for the words, "Stretch it out." So when they were supposed to stretch out the sounds of a word, they were supposed to pull their hands apart. 3. Then there is a guided practice time, when the children practice writing some words as they sound on white boards with dry erase markers. I let the children generate the words that they wanted to write, but I did ask them to tell me words about what Santa's elves could do, because we had started talking about elves the day before and made our elf project! (See my last year's blog entry on how to make that super cute elf!) 4. I waited until the next day to do the independent practice portion of the lesson, which is having the children write a sentence or two using sight words that they already know how to spell and also some new words that they have to sound out. 5. Of course, closing the lesson with the usual questions such as "What did we learn today?" "What's the Big Idea? "Who can tell me the steps?" is always a good thing, but somehow I seem to always run out of time for that sort of thing! But I know that it is good for their language development, and it is my lowest children that really need this the most, unfortunately. They really need just as much language as I can possibly "feed" them on a continual basis. And so for that reason, I am willing to try to remember to do it- but I will admit that I don't feel so bad if I forget and leave it out of the high kids' lessons! One problem that I encountered with the lesson was trying to get my students to cooperate and really try to stretch out the sounds, rather than just try to guess at the sounds that they thought were there once I said the word. Some of them were a little over-confident and just wanted to write the whole word immediately. SO..... as I have done in the past, I got out my trusty ball box! I cleared the things off of the table that were not needed for the lesson right away, and started rolling the ball to the children that were following my directions. "I like the way So-and-So is stretching out the sounds like I asked, and ONLY writing the first sound, just like I asked him to!" I just kept rolling the ball back and forth to the children that were staying right with me, and NOT rolling it to the ones that were ignoring my directions and writing whatever they pleased, and drawing pictures on the white boards. I soon had everyone following my directions except for one little boy, who was clearly upset that he was not getting a ball, but still not wanting to change his behavior to get the ball. I just kept repeating what he had to do to get it, and finally he started to comply! Soon, the entire group was happily cooperating and doing their best to stretch out and then write each of the individual sounds. Hooray! This was my lowest group, so this was no small accomplishment. :) |Sounds Fun Card for the "Ch" Sound| (It goes "Chug, chug!) |These are my teacher made sample clay snowmen.| Teaching children to make snowmen out of clay is really not that hard! But you will need access to a kiln and about ten to fifteen pounds of white clay for a class of twenty-four children. (Do not buy the red clay for white snowmen!) You will also need at least one week for the snowmen to dry before they can be fired in the kiln, and then another day to glaze them in the classroom before they can be fired again. So the absolute minimum amount of time needed to complete this clay project is a week and a half, but it can be completed more comfortably in two weeks. I always try to give myself a month, just so that if anyone is absent on the day that we make the clay project, I have a few days to make it up, and then plenty of the "wiggle room" I always need to find the time to get the snowmen loaded and unloaded in the kiln. At my school, we also have to take into account that there may be other teachers using the kiln as well, so it's best not to put things like this off til the last minute. |Child-Made Clay Snowmen, Waiting to Be Glazed| The snowmen in the picture above the title for this section are my teacher made samples. The snowmen in the picture to the left that are still to be glazed are strictly child made, and I think you can really see the difference. I think that the child made ones are really adorable! The snowmen below with the hats on them were made by the class next door. They have a retired teacher come in and do them with the children one at a time. You can see that these snowmen have had a LOT more adult intervention, and they all tend to come out looking the same. In fact, it is hard to tell that a child ever laid hands on them, in my opinion! I really respect this particular retired teacher, but she and I do not share the same opinion about children's artwork, and especially about clay projects. I really want my students' gifts of clay artwork to look like their work at the age of five, not the teacher's work when they were five. The fact that the child made it him or herself is what makes the gift special, and it should be treasured as such! Behind the three finished sample snowmen that I made, you can see the whole class' snowmen, ready to be fired. |Snowmen Made with One-On-One Teacher Help| So, the children score the clay and put the slip between all of the balls of clay. Then they use the fat eraser side of a pencil to poke holes in the sides of the snowman for the arms. (The arm holes only need to be about 1/4 inch long.) Then they roll some primary pencil sized "snakes" for the arms and put some slip inside the arm holes. Then they put the arms inside the arm holes. The arms should be no longer than an inch or two, and about the width of a fat primary pencil. Then they use the sharpened side of a pencil to make the snowman's face. They use the eraser side for the buttons. If you want to add a hat, then they need to roll out another ball of clay, and then squish it flat with the palm of their hand, but no thinner than 1/4 of an inch or it will be too fragile. Score it and add some slip, and do the same to the head, and then put it on the snowman's head. That will be the brim of the hat. Then add another ball of clay to the top of the flattened clay, and that will form the "stove-pipe" part of the top of the hat. Of course, that has to be scored and it needs slip in order for it to stick. |My Snowmen in Front, Drying Student Snowmen in Back| Here are another couple of tips. I NEVER let them scratch their names into the bottom of the snowman, because very often they push too hard and ruin the whole thing by squishing it and have to start over. If you don't have time to get to everybody, have them write their names on a paper towel, and have them leave their snowmen on the paper towel with their name on it. I ALWAYS make a couple of extra snowmen, and I just scratch an X lightly on the bottom. Somebody's snowman will probably break in the kiln, and somebody is going to be heartbroken. All it takes is one little air bubble inside the middle of one snowman, and that air will expand and the snowman will explode in the kiln! BUT... I can usually substitute my snowman for theirs and just tell them that their name got rubbed off, and they never know. Also, if you get a new student, there will be a snowman for that child when it's time to glaze them. By the way, if your snowmen seem large and heavy to you, and you are afraid that they might have air bubbles or something like that in them, you can always stick the fat eraser side of a pencil up the bottom of the middle of the snowman to help it dry. BUT, this must be done while they are still wet, of course. Hint: if you make 24 snowmen out of ten pounds of clay, then they will probably dry just fine without the extra hole in the bottom middle. If your snowmen are a lot bigger than that, then you may need to poke that extra hole in them to help them dry. Watch out for the names, though! You'll have to write the names in the bottom of the snowmen going around the edges of them rather than across the middle. The snowmen have to air dry for about a week before going into the kiln to be fired at Cone 4. Then they have to be glazed, and we usually fire them at Cone 5 or 6, depending on the glaze. If you are not sure, just look at the bottle of glaze and it will tell you what temperature to fire them at. I always go with the higher of the temperatures. Of course, they can't be glazed on the bottom of the snowmen, or they stick to the kiln and you have to break them to get them off. I always lay them down flat on their backs in a tub on some dish towels when I am ready to take them over to the kiln, because if one of them tips over when they are standing up, then a whole bunch of them tip over, domino style. They are at their most fragile at this point, when they are ready for their first firing. Also, I always dry them way up high OUT OF REACH of the children, because the temptation to lift them up to read the names and check to see which snowman is theirs is totally overwhelming! They lift them up, touch them, and break them. And sadly, the one they break is almost NEVER theirs! So I put mine up on top of a cupboard where nobody, (not even a volunteer,) can easily touch them to investigate. |Upon firing, the snowman on the right's arms fell off| because they were too long To wrap our gifts, the children sponge paint a wreath on a white lunch bag, and use a Q-tip to put on holly berries. Then we wrap the snowmen in tissue paper and send them home, with lots of stern reminders about the hazards of stuffing them in their backpacks and then throwing the backpacks on the ground. If possible, I prefer to hand these treasures straight to into the hand of the adult that picks them up after school! If you decide to make these clay snowmen, I would love to hear from you! Have fun! You always have an excuse for not giving me a kiss! Click on all of the bowling pins that meet the criteria. For example, if the message reads "Click on all pins greater than 7 , you would click on all of the bowling pins that had a problem that equaled a number greater than 7. Then click "Roll Now". The object of the game is to knock down as many pins as possible in ten frames. Be mindful of the timer! Step 3.) The game reveals how accurate your clicks were. Green pins are those that were supposed to be clicked, and red pins are those that were not supposed to be clicked. Dull pins are those that were clicked. Title: How to Deal with Family: A Comprehensive Guide Introduction: Family relationships can be complicated and challenging at times. Dealing with family members requires patience, understanding, and effective communication skills. This guide will provide you with detailed steps, helpful tips, and crucial guidelines to help you navigate through difficult family situations while maintaining healthy and loving relationships. Step 1: Understand Your Family Dynamics Understanding your family dynamics is essential when dealing with family issues. Analyze your role within the family system, identify patterns of behavior, and recognize any triggers that may lead to conflicts. By doing this, you'll become more aware of the underlying causes of problems and find ways to address them constructively. Key Tips: * Consider seeking professional guidance from a therapist or counselor who specializes in family dynamics. * Reflect on past experiences to understand how they have shaped current familial relationships. Step 2: Set Boundaries Establishing clear boundaries ensures respect for individual needs and promotes healthier interactions. Communicate openly about what behaviors are acceptable and unacceptable, both emotionally and physically. Maintaining these limits will foster positive connections and minimize potential misunderstandings. Key Guidelines: * Be consistent in enforcing established boundaries. * Clearly express expectations without blaming or criticizing others. Step 3: Practice Active Listening Effective communication begins with active listening. Give undivided attention to family members during conversations, show empathy, and validate their feelings. By actively engaging in dialogue, you demonstrate respect and encourage openness between all parties involved. Key Tips: * Avoid interrupting or talking over someone else. * Paraphrase what you heard to ensure accurate understanding. Step 4: Use "I" Statements Instead of Blameful Language When addressing concerns, utilize "I" statements instead of accusatory language. Describe specific actions and emotions rather than attacking character traits. This approach encourages productive conversation and reduces defensiveness. Key Example: Instead of saying, "You never listen to me!" try stating, "I feel ignored when I speak and would appreciate if you could give me your full attention." Step 5: Manage Conflict Resolution Conflicts arise even in close-knit families. When disagreements occur, maintain composure, remain calm, and focus on finding solutions. Keep discussions solution-oriented by focusing on common goals rather than assigning blame. Key Tips: * Take breaks if tensions rise too high. * Compromise whenever possible. Step 6: Seek Outside Support If Needed In some cases, outside assistance might be necessary to resolve complex family issues. Reach out to trusted friends, support groups, therapists, or religious leaders for additional guidance and perspective. Key Guidelines: * Choose individuals who possess strong interpersonal skills and proven experience working through similar challenges. * Remember that seeking help is a sign of strength, not weakness. Step 7: Foster Healthy Relationships Through Quality Time Together Strengthen bonds among family members by dedicating time to shared activities and interests. Regularly scheduled gatherings promote positivity and create lasting memories. Key Tips: * Encourage participation from everyone, regardless of age or ability level. * Rotate responsibility for planning events to keep things fresh and exciting. Conclusion: Navigating family relationships can be challenging yet rewarding. Implementing these strategies fosters an environment conducive to growth, healing, and love. As you practice these techniques consistently, dealing with family becomes less daunting and far more enjoyable. Once upon a time, there was a grumpy bear. He didn't like to play, he just liked to be alone. One day, he decided he wanted to try something new. He decided to the swing in the park. He hopped onto the swing and pushed off the ground. Summary:A fantastic printable game to help your students reinforce the phonetic sounds, 'ai', 'ay', 'ee' and 'ea'. Feel free to download it, print it and play! Printable Phonics Game ai, ay, ee & ea found in this game... Print the cards, laminate them if possible and cut them out. How to play: 1. Two, three or four players sit so they are facing each other. 2. Select a dealer to shuffle the cards and give five cards to each member of the group, as well as themselves. 3. Place all the remaining cards in a pile face down in the centre. Next flip one card off the top of this pile and place it face up next to the stack. 4. The dealer reads the card by pronouncing the sound of the letters marked in red and then the word e.g. If the card is 'rain' the dealer says, ' a like in rain.' If the card is ' eat ' the dealer says, ' e like in 'eat.' 5. The children then look at their cards and take their turn moving to the left around the circle. If they have cards with the same sound as the one turned over by the dealer they can place one on top of the pile by saying ' a like in train' or ' a like in gain '. Same sounds in blue can count as same suit: eg. ay may be put on top of ai. 6. If they do not have a match in their hand they pick up a card from the facedown pile. If the card they pick up is a match they can put it straight down, if not, they place it in their hand and the next player has a turn. If the player does not have a match but has a CHANGE card in their hand they can change the sound being matched i.e. if they had the cards ' stream and steam ' they would place down their CHANGE card and say "I'd like to change it to e like in stream". All the following players must match for ee or ea.. 8. The person who is the first to have no cards left is the winner. NB The power of this game as a learning tool comes from having the children say ' ay like in crayon' when playing. Please ensure they keep doing it throughout the learning Shall we take your bathing cap off now? You also find the phrase "Ola kala" in Greek, which means something like "Everything fine". Polynomial functions are useful when solving problems that ask us to find things like maximum income, revenue or production quantities. Finding maximum and minimum values of polynomial functions help us solve these types of problems. When setting up these functions, we first determine what the problems is asking us to maximize and then set up the function accordingly. So we've got some word problems I want to talk about. And these are specifically optimization problems, and that just means that we're looking for the maximum or minimum value of some function. And in this case we'll deal with polynomial functions mostly. Let's read this one. It says: "A farmer currently has 200 crates of apples and can harvest an additional 10 crates per day. The current price of apples is $120 per crate and is expected to drop $4 each day. When should the farmer sell to maximize her income?" Okay. Well, let's back up for a second. Income. That's going to be our function. And that's what we want to maximize. We want to maximize income. And we want to maximize over time, when should she sell. So -- and the variable is going to be the number of days. So income. Income is going to be the number of crates she sells, times the price per crate. Now, first of all, I need to get a function for each of these guys, the number of crates. And the problem says that she has 200. 200 crates. And that she can harvest an additional 10 per day. Let me write that as plus 10X. The minute I do, I've identified the variable X as the number of days. So here X is the number of days she waits. So, for example, if she doesn't wait at all, 0 days, then she'll have 200 crates. Now, the price per crate, this guy, that's also going to be a function of X. It's going to be -- well now the price is $120 and the price drops by $4 each day. So minus 4X. And that means that the income function, let's call it I of X, is the number of crates, 200 plus 10X times the price per crate, 120 minus 4X. Now, this is a quadratic function. And the great thing about quadratic functions is we know exactly where the maximum is going to occur. It's going to occur at the vertex. So we're going to make use of that fact. Now, here if you look at this equation, I can actually tell where the X intercepts of its graph would be. So I'm actually going to graph this function. 1X intercept is going to be when 200 plus 10X equals 0. 200 plus 10X equals 0. That means 10 X equals negative 200, so X equals negative 20. So one intercept is going to be at negative 20. Let me mark that. Negative 10. Negative 20. That's the intercept. And then another one will come from this factor. When does 4X equal 120? When X equals 30. So there's another intercept. Positive 30. Now, one thing I need to think about is what values of X makes sense in this case? We're not just dealing with a function in the abstract sense. We're dealing with a function that describes income for this farmer after she's waited X days. X can't be negative. So we should probably specify that X is greater than or equal to 0. All right. Now let me draw a rough graph of this quadratic. It's going to be a downward opening quadratic. We can tell that because the leading coefficient would be negative 40 X squared. So let me just draw this. And because we're not really concerned with the negative part of this graph, right, that would represent a negative number of days, which doesn't make sense, I'm just going to dash this part of the graph. We're also not really concerned with this part. Now, why would you think that is? Here, the income's negative, right? I'm sure she wants to stay away from this, and of course it doesn't actually make any sense. It represents a time when the price is actually negative. That doesn't make sense. We'll just stay between 0 and 30. And remember the maximum is going to occur right where the vertex is. Now, the vertex, how are we going to find that? Well, parabolas have the marvelous quality that the vertex is exactly halfway between the intercepts. The vertex, we'll call it X max, the X coordinate where the maximum occurs is going to be the average of these two. Negative 20 plus 30 over 2. That's 10 over 2 or 5. And that's it. The problem only asked for us to find the number of days that she should wait. So that her income would be the maximum. 5's the answer. So she should wait five days. Now, if you're curious about how much she would make, you can easily calculate that by plugging 5 back into the function. And even though the problem doesn't call for it, I'm kind of curious. Let's find income of 5. We have 200 plus 10 times 5, and we have 120 minus 4 times 5. So that's 250. 100. 25,000. She'll make $25,000. yeah, say wait 'til I get my first tooth we're going to be doing a lot of damage. Going after the one with chocolate fudge. Chi you got the truck over there. Becoming a pharmacy technician in Alaska requires completing specific educational and certification requirements. This article will provide an in-depth guide on how to become a pharmacy technician in Alaska, including key tips and guidelines to help you achieve your goal with ease. Step 1: Meet the Basic Requirements To become a pharmacy technician in Alaska, you must meet the following basic requirements: * You must be at least 18 years old. * Have a high school diploma or GED equivalent. * Possess good moral character, which includes having no felony convictions related to controlled substances. Meeting these requirements ensures that you are eligible to enroll in a pharmacy technician training program and take the necessary certifications required by the state. Tip: It is essential to maintain a clean criminal record throughout your career as a pharmacy technician since any conviction related to controlled substances may affect your ability to work in this field. Step 2: Complete a Pharmacy Technician Training Program Alaska does not require aspiring pharmacy technicians to complete formal education before they can practice; however, pursuing accredited training programs can enhance one's knowledge about the profession and improve job prospects. These programs typically cover topics such as medication preparation, drug classifications, inventory management, and customer service skills. They also offer hands-on experience under the supervision of licensed pharmacists. Ensure that the chosen institution is accredited by reputable organizations like the American Society of Health-System Pharmacists (ASHP) or the Accreditation Council for Pharmacy Education (ACPE). Completing an accredited course shows potential employers that you have received quality instruction and prepares you better for national certification examinations. Step 3: Get Certified While Alaska doesn't mandate licensure for practicing as a pharmacy technician, most employers prefer hiring certified individuals due to their demonstrated competency. There are two main nationally recognized certifications available: 1. Certified Pharmacy Technician (CPhT): Offered by the Pharmacy Technician Certification Board (PTCB), candidates need to pass a comprehensive examination covering various aspects of pharmacy operations. To qualify, applicants should either graduate from an ASHP-accredited pharmacy technician program or acquire 500 hours of work experience within six months of application submission. Title: How to Hook Two Bracelets Together Introduction: Joining two bracelets together can be a fun and creative way to customize your accessories. This guide will walk you through the process of hooking two bracelets together using simple steps. Whether you're looking to create a unique layered look or want to repair a broken clasp, this tutorial has got you covered! Things You'll Need: 1. Two bracelets with compatible clasps (e.g., lobster clasps, toggle clasps) 2. Wire cutters or scissors (for cutting jump rings if necessary) 3. Pliers (optional but recommended for easier manipulation) Instructions: Step 1: Choose Compatible Bracelets Ensure that both bracelets have compatible clasps; otherwise, they won't connect properly. Common compatible clasps include lobster clasps and toggle clasps. If one bracelet has a different type of clasp, consider replacing it with a similar one before proceeding. Key Tip: When choosing which bracelets to link, make sure their combined weight is manageable and comfortable on your wrist. Overloading a weak clasp may cause it to break prematurely. Step 2: Prepare Your Workspace Find a clean, well-lit area where you can work comfortably without distractions. Lay out your materials so they are easy to access during the process. Guideline: It might help to place a small piece of cloth or paper towel under your workspace to catch any falling components. Step 3: Connect the Clasps If both bracelets have lobster clasps, open one by gently squeezing its side with pliers or your fingers. Be careful not to damage the shape of the clasp while doing so. Once opened, attach it to the other bracelet's clasp securely. Close the joined clasp by squeezing it shut again with pliers or your fingers. If one bracelet has a toggle clasp, simply slide the toggle bar through the loop on the other bracelet to connect them. Make sure the connection feels sturdy and doesn't wobble excessively. Key Tips: - For lobster clasps, remember to squeeze only the curved sides when opening and closing them - never attempt to pull apart the straight ends as this could weaken or damage the mechanism. - Always ensure that connected clasps are fully closed and secured to prevent accidental unfastening. Step 4: Test the Connection Put on the linked bracelets and move your arm around gently to check whether they stay connected. The bracelets should feel snug and stable on your wrist without sliding or twisting too much. Guideline: Adjust the length of either bracelet if needed by adding or removing links to achieve optimal comfort and fit. Step 5: Secure Any Jump Rings (Optional) If you had to add a jump ring to connect the bracelets, double-check that it is tightly closed. Use pliers to press the ends of the jump ring together until they meet seamlessly. Key Tip: To avoid damaging the jump ring, grasp it with two pairs of pliers at opposite ends and twist them in opposing directions to close the gap. Conclusion: You now know how to hook two bracelets together! By following these clear and practical steps, you can confidently combine your favorite pieces into stylish combinations or temporarily fix broken clasps. Enjoy showing off your creativity and resourcefulness with your newly linked bracelets! This is also very soft and chewy. The problem is that recently, the greenhouse effect has become stronger. This is because humans have been using large amounts of fossil fuels, which release carbon dioxide when they are burned. Title: How to Get a Longer Caption on Snapchat Snapchat is a popular multimedia messaging app that allows users to send photos, videos, and messages (known as "snaps") to their friends and followers. One feature of Snapchat is the ability to add captions to your snaps, which can be up to 80 characters long by default. However, there may be times when you want to include a longer caption on your snap. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to do just that: 1. Open the Snapchat app on your device. If you haven't already logged in, enter your username or email address and password to log in. 2. Take a photo or video snap using the camera button at the bottom of the screen. You can also choose an existing image from your camera roll by swiping up on the camera screen and selecting the image you want to use. 3. Tap the text icon (represented by a 'T') at the top right corner of the screen to open the caption box. This will bring up a keyboard where you can type out your caption. 4. Type out your caption in the caption box. Keep in mind that the standard character limit for captions is 80 characters. To get around this limitation, you can use special characters to combine multiple words into one. For example, instead of typing "hi there," you could type "hi+there" to save space. 5. Once you have typed out your caption, press and hold the blue "Send To" button at the bottom of the screen. This will bring up additional options for customizing your snap. 6. Select the "More..." option. This will take you to a new menu with more advanced settings for your snap. 7. Scroll down until you see the "Caption Characters" setting. By default, this should be set to "Standard." To increase the character limit for your caption, tap on the "Standard" button and select "Long" instead. 8. With the "Long" option selected, you now have a much larger character limit for your caption. Go back to the caption box and continue typing out your message. 9. When you are satisfied with your caption, tap the "Send To" button to send your snap to your chosen recipients. Key Tips and Guidelines: * Be aware of the difference between single-width and double-width characters. Single-width characters (such as letters and numbers) take up less space than double-width characters (such as emojis). Use single-width characters whenever possible to maximize the length of your caption. * Consider breaking up your caption into multiple parts if necessary. While it would be ideal to fit everything into one continuous caption, sometimes it's simply not possible due to the character limitations. Instead, try splitting your caption into two or three separate sections. Not only does this make it easier to read, but it also gives you more room to express yourself. * Remember that longer captions can be overwhelming, so keep them concise and easy to understand. Avoid using unnecessary words or phrases, and focus on getting straight to the point. Your goal should be to convey your message clearly and effectively while still staying within the character limits. * Practice makes perfect! The more you experiment with different techniques for increasing your caption length, the better you will become at crafting compelling and engaging captions. Don't be afraid to try new things and push the boundaries of what's possible on Snapchat. By following these steps and guidelines, you should now be able to create longer captions on Snapchat without any issues. Happy snapping! Did you pass you did a good job? How to Perform the Heimlich Maneuver on an Infant =================================================== The Heimlich maneuver is a standard first-aid procedure that can save a choking victim's life by dislodging the obstructing object from their windpipe. This technique differs slightly when performed on infants under one year old due to their smaller size and unique anatomy. The following steps will guide you through performing the infant version of the Heimlich maneuver safely and effectively: **Step 1:** Identify signs of choking ----------------------------------- Before attempting any first aid techniques, ensure the situation calls for intervention. Signs of choking include sudden coughing or gagging, difficulty breathing, skin, lips, or nails turning blue or dusky, and loss of consciousness. If the child is crying, making sounds, or able to breathe, they are likely not choking and may only have something stuck in their mouth. **Step 2:** Place the infant face down on your forearm ---------------------------------------------------- Support the infant's head with your hand, ensuring their airway remains open. Position them so their chin rests on your arm, stomach-down. Your other hand should be free to deliver back blows. Make sure you are using adequate support to avoid dropping or injuring the baby during this process. ![Placing the infant on your forearm]( "I'm Brownell from the Alton Chemical Company. I happened to be this way on a business trip and thought I'd drop in to discuss that matter which I wrote you about some time ago. By the way, we didn't hear from you. " Anne looked embarrassed and said hesitantly: "I hadn't had time to write. You see, the formula--" she trailed off as Madge gave her a warning look. And then you gave her the whole thing. Can I sleep on you like a pillow? Did you help me eat my french fries? Delaware () is a state in the United States. It is sometimes called the "First State" because it was the first colony to accept the new constitution in 1787. Its capital is Dover and its biggest city is Wilmington. It is the second smallest state in the United States. The World Wide Web ("WWW" or "The Web") is the part of the Internet that contains websites and webpages. It was invented in 1989 by Tim Berners-Lee at CERN, Geneva, Switzerland. Sir Tim Berners-Lee created a new markup language called HTML. Websites are composed of pages linked by hypertext links that are written in HTML. What's that new song you were singin' this mornin'? Title: How to Build a Homemade Swing Building a homemade swing can be a fun and rewarding DIY project that allows you to create a unique addition to your backyard or porch. This guide will provide clear, step-by-step instructions on constructing a basic wooden A-frame swing set with a simple plank seat. The tools and materials required are accessible at most home improvement stores, making this an ideal weekend project for those who enjoy working with wood and other building materials. **Materials Needed:** 1. Four 4x4 pressure-treated posts (8 feet long) - serve as the main support beams 2. Two 2x6 boards (10 feet long) - form the crossbeam and top beam of the A-frame 3. One 2x8 board (12 feet long) - serves as the actual swing seat 4. Twenty-four inch galvanized lag screws (3 inches long) - secure the frame together 5. Sixteen inch x 3 inch eyebolts with nuts and washers - attach the ropes to the swing set 6. Eight pieces of rope ( inch diameter, approximately 10 feet long) - suspend the swing from tree branches or overhead beams 7. Sandpaper - smooth out rough edges and splinters 8. Wood stain or paint - customize the look of your swing set 9. Stain or paintbrushes - apply the finish to the wood 10. Saw - cut lumber to size 11. Drill - predrill holes for screws and bolts 12. Tape measure - take accurate measurements throughout the process 1 XXX I eat it I will and you a tomato. Title: How to Change the Appearance of Your Toolbar in Zoner Photo Studio Zoner Photo Studio is a powerful photo editing software that offers users a wide range of tools to enhance their photos. One customizable feature in Zoner Photo Studio is the toolbar, which allows you to personalize your workspace by adding or removing buttons according to your needs. This tutorial will guide you through changing the appearance of your toolbar in Zoner Photo Studio. **Step 1: Open Zoner Photo Studio** Start by launching Zoner Photo Studio on your computer. Once opened, you'll be greeted with the default interface. **Step 2: Access the Customization Menu** To change the appearance of your toolbar, first make sure you are in the Manager module (the button with house icon). Then look at the top right corner of the screen - there should be three vertical dots representing the 'Customize' menu. Click on these dots. A dropdown list will appear; select 'Customize Interface.' **Step 3: Choose the Toolbars Tab** In the new window that opens up, click on the 'Toolbars' tab located near the bottom left corner of the dialog box. Here, you can see all available toolbars and manage them as needed. **Step 4: Select the Desired Toolbar** From the list of displayed toolbars, choose the one you want to modify. For instance, if you wish to alter the Standard toolbar, simply tick the checkbox next to its name. If you want to create a new toolbar, click on 'New Toolbar,' give it a suitable name, and then proceed. **Step 5: Add or Remove Buttons** After selecting the toolbar, its current configuration will load in the large pane above the list of toolbars. To add more functionalities, find an unused command from the 'Commands' category on the left side of the dialog box. Drag this command into the toolbar where you desire it to be placed. Conversely, to remove any existing button, just drag it out of the toolbar back onto the 'Toolbar Commands' panel. **Key Tips:** - When rearranging buttons within a toolbar, remember that they snap into position when moved close to other buttons. - You can reset a toolbar to its original state by right-clicking it and choosing 'Reset.' - Keep your most frequently used commands close together for easy access. **Step 6: Confirm Changes** Once satisfied with your modifications, press 'OK' at the bottom right of the dialog box to save changes and return to the main application view. The updated toolbar should now be visible. **Step 7: Experiment and Refine** Remember, modifying your toolbar layout is a process of experimentation. Feel free to try different arrangements until you find what works best for you. Always keep usability in mind while making adjustments - having too many buttons may clutter the space, whereas too few might require excessive navigation. By following this comprehensive guide, you have successfully changed the appearance of your toolbar in Zoner Photo Studio! Enjoy optimizing your workflow with your newly configured workspace. Title: How to Drive a Motorbike Introduction: Driving a motorbike can be an exhilarating experience, offering a sense of freedom and excitement that is hard to match. However, it's important to remember that operating a motorcycle requires skill, knowledge, and responsibility. This comprehensive guide will walk you through the necessary steps to safely drive a motorbike, providing in-depth explanations and helpful tips along the way. I. Preparing to Ride 1. Obtain a valid motorcycle license or permit: Before you start riding, ensure you have the proper authorization from your local Department of Motor Vehicles (DMV) or equivalent organization. You may need to complete a written test, skills test, and vision exam. Key tip: Practice safe driving habits while holding a learner's permit to build confidence and demonstrate competence before taking the skills test. 2. Choose the right bike: Select a motorcycle that fits your size, skill level, and purpose. A smaller engine capacity might be more suitable if you are a beginner rider. Research various models, read reviews, and consult with experienced riders. Key guideline: Always wear appropriate safety gear when testing ride different bikes to minimize injury risk during trial runs. 3. Invest in quality safety equipment: Purchase well-fitted helmets, jackets, gloves, pants, and boots designed specifically for motorcyclists. High-quality gear provides better protection against injuries in case of accidents. Key tip: Look for CE or Snell certification labels on helmets as they meet strict safety standards. II. Basic Motorbike Operation 4. Familiarize yourself with the controls: Spend time understanding the functions of the clutch lever, front brake lever, rear brake pedal, throttle grip, gear shift lever, and choke (if applicable). Key tip: Memorize the location of all switches, buttons, and indicators on the dashboard for easy access during operation. 5. Perform pre-ride checks: Examine the tire pressure, tread depth, fluid levels, lights, signals, mirrors, and horn before every ride. Address any issues found before setting off. Key guideline: Develop a mental checklist to ensure consistency and thoroughness in inspections. III. Bury Your Burdens: A Comprehensive Step-by-Step Guide Introduction: Life can be overwhelming at times, leaving us feeling weighed down by our burdens. It's essential to find healthy ways to cope with these feelings and release them from our minds and hearts. One such method is burying your burdens, which involves symbolically letting go of negative emotions or experiences. This guide will walk you through this process, providing detailed steps, helpful tips, and crucial guidelines. Step 1: Identify Your Burdens Before you can bury your burdens, you need to identify what they are. Take some time to reflect on what has been causing you stress, anxiety, sadness, anger, or any other negative emotion. These burdens could be related to relationships, work, finances, health, or personal losses. Be specific when naming your burdens; this will help you focus your energy during the burial ceremony. Key Tip: Writing down your burdens can make the identification process more tangible and help clarify your thoughts. Why it works: By acknowledging your burdens, you take the first step toward addressing them and finding peace. Step 2: Choose a Symbolic Object Select an object that represents the burden(s) you want to bury. This object should have personal significance and evoke strong emotional connections to the issue at hand. For example, if your burden is related to a failed relationship, you might choose a broken heart necklace or a photograph of the two of you together. Guideline: Avoid using living things as symbols; instead, opt for inanimate objects like stones, paper, or fabric. Why it works: Having a physical representation of your burden allows you to let go of it more effectively during the burial ceremony. [BEF knocks over zoo fence with dinosaur] BXR is a powerful technique used in the video game Halo 2 that allows players to quickly switch between their weapons, melee an enemy, and then fire their weapon again. This move can give players a significant advantage in combat by catching opponents off guard and dealing extra damage. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to execute the BXR technique: **Step 1: Select your preferred weapons** To successfully perform the BXR, you will need to have two specific weapons equipped: a Battle Rifle (BR) and an Energy Sword (ES). The BR serves as your primary weapon for firing and switching, while the ES is used for the melee attack. Make sure both weapons are fully charged before attempting the BXR. *Key Tip*: Familiarize yourself with the controls for quickly swapping weapons. On the Xbox controller, this is typically done by pressing the 'Y' button. **Step 2: Approach an opponent** Wait for an opportunity where an enemy is within melee range (about arm's length). Ideally, they should be distracted or focused on another target, making them less likely to anticipate your attack. *Guideline*: Practice patience and wait for the right moment. A poorly timed BXR may leave you vulnerable to counterattacks. **Step 3: Initiate the BXR** As you approach your opponent, press and hold the left trigger to aim down sights with the BR. Simultaneously press the 'A' button to throw a grenade at their feet. This step is optional but can help disorient the enemy and increase your chances of success. *Key Tip*: Throwing a grenade isn't always necessary, but it can add an element of surprise and make the BXR more effective. **Step 4: Perform the weapon swap** Immediately after throwing the grenade, release the left trigger and press the 'Y' button to switch from the BR to the ES. Be quick and precise with this action; ideally, you want the weapon swap to occur during the animation when your character holsters the BR. *Guideline*: Timing is crucial here. With practice, you'll learn the optimal timing for executing the weapon swap smoothly. **Step 5: Melee the enemy** Once the weapon swap is complete, immediately press the 'X' button to initiate a melee attack with the ES. Your character should lunge forward and strike the enemy with the sword. *Key Tip*: Make sure to aim the reticle at your opponent before performing the melee attack. Properly aligning your crosshair increases the likelihood of landing a successful hit. **Step 6: Fire your weapon** After the melee strike, quickly press the left trigger again to draw your BR and resume firing. If executed correctly, you should catch your opponent off guard and deal substantial damage. *Guideline*: Remember to time your shots carefully. Following up with accurate gunfire can secure a kill or force the enemy into retreat. In conclusion, mastering the BXR technique requires patience, precision, and plenty of practice. By following these steps and focusing on perfecting your timing, you can become a formidable force in Halo 2 multiplayer matches. Good luck! Anne Frank was born on 12 June 1929 in Frankfurt, Germany. Title: How to Perform Side Crunches with an Exercise Ball Side crunches using an exercise ball are a great way to target your oblique muscles and add variety to your abdominal workout routine. This exercise can help improve core strength, stability, and overall fitness level when performed correctly and consistently. Here's a comprehensive guide on how to do side crunches with an exercise ball safely and effectively. **Things you will need:** * An exercise ball (also known as a Swiss ball or stability ball) * Comfortable clothing that allows for movement * Suitable footwear for exercising **Step 1: Selecting the right exercise ball** Select an exercise ball that is appropriate for your height and weight range. As a general guideline, choose a ball where your knees form a 90-degree angle when sitting on top of it while keeping your feet flat on the floor. Properly inflate the ball according to manufacturer recommendations. **Step 2: Preparation** Wear comfortable clothes and supportive shoes before starting your workout. Clear enough space around you to perform the exercise without any obstructions or risks of falling over. Place the exercise ball nearby so you can access it quickly once you begin. **Step 3: Familiarize yourself with proper posture** Stand next to the exercise ball and place both hands on its surface. Lean forward slightly until your upper body forms about a 45-degree angle relative to the ground. Keep your back straight and engage your abs during this process. Practice shifting your weight from one leg to another, maintaining balance throughout the motion. **Step 4: Positioning yourself on the exercise ball** Place the exercise ball on the ground and position yourself beside it. Carefully roll onto the ball, placing one hip against it while keeping your other foot firmly planted on the ground. Your torso should be perpendicular to the floor, forming a 'T' shape. Extend your arms outwards at shoulder height for added support and balance. **Step 5: Engaging your core and performing the side crunch** Take a deep breath and exhale slowly as you contract your obliques, lifting your hips off the ball and bringing your elbow towards your opposite knee. Focus on using your core muscles rather than relying solely on momentum or arm strength. Hold the contraction briefly at the peak of the movement, then lower yourself back down to the starting position under control. Complete 8-12 repetitions on each side. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** * Maintain neutral spine alignment by avoiding excessive arching or rounding of your back during the exercise. * Use controlled movements instead of swinging motions; focus on feeling the burn in your obliques rather than rushing through the set. * Breathe naturally and evenly throughout the entire exercise. Avoid holding your breath, especially during exertion phases. * If you find balancing challenging initially, consider doing side crunches without the exercise ball or try standing closer to a wall or sturdy object for additional support. * Gradually increase the difficulty by adding more sets, repetitions, or resistance bands wrapped around your knees. * Remember to warm up properly before attempting any new exercises and cool down afterwards to prevent injury and promote recovery. Lily liked to create things with her crayons and paper. She made pictures of flowers, stars, and animals. One day, she wanted to create something new. She looked at a book and saw a picture of a shrimp. It was pink and had many legs and a long tail. Lily thought it looked funny and cute. She took a pink crayon and started to draw a shrimp on her paper. She gave it big eyes, a smile, and some dots. She was happy with her creation. She showed it to her mom and said, "Look, mom, I made a shrimp!" Mom smiled and said, "That's very nice, Lily. What is your shrimp's name?" Lily thought for a moment and said, "His name is Bob. He is a friendly shrimp." Mom nodded and said, "That's a good name. Do you want to hang your shrimp on the fridge?" Lily nodded and said, "Yes, please. I want everyone to see Bob." Mom helped Lily to hang her shrimp on the fridge with a magnet. Lily was proud of her shrimp. She said, "Bye, Bob. I'll see you later." But when Lily came back from playing outside, she saw that her shrimp was gone. She looked around and saw her brother, Tom, holding her paper. He had torn it into pieces and was laughing. He said, "Your shrimp is silly and ugly. I don't like it. I made it better." Lily felt sad and angry. She said, "No, you didn't. You ruined it. That was my shrimp. His name was Bob. He was friendly. You are mean and fierce. Give me back my shrimp." Tom said, "No, I won't. It's mine now. Go away." Lily ran to mom and told her what Tom did. Mom was not happy. She said, "Tom, that was not nice. You should not take and destroy Lily's things. You should apologize and make it up to her." Tom said, "But I don't like her shrimp. It's stupid." Mom said, "It doesn't matter if you like it or not. It was her creation. She worked hard on it and she loved it. You should respect her feelings and her work. How would you feel if someone did that to your things?" Tom thought for a while and realized that he would feel bad if someone did that to his things. He said, "I'm sorry, Lily. I was wrong. I shouldn't have taken and torn your shrimp. Can I help you make a new one?" Lily said, "It's okay, Tom. I forgive you. Yes, you can help me make a new one. Maybe we can make a friend for Bob." Tom said, "Okay, let's do that. What should we name him?" Lily said, "How about Tim?" Tom said, "That sounds good. Let's go get some crayons and paper." Mom smiled and said, "That's very nice of you, Tom. I'm proud of you. And I'm proud of you, Lily. You are very creative and kind. You both learned a valuable lesson today. You should always treat others the way you want to be treated." The end. Practicing Pronunciation through ProverbsYi Yang yangyi [at] gse.harvard.edu Sentences with several words involving the same sound are good materials for practicing that sound. Many proverbs contain the rhetorical devices related to sound such as alliteration, rhyme and repetition, and thus very suitable for pronunciation exercises (For instance: Practice makes perfect. / Where there is a will, there is a way.) Repeating a sound two or more times in a short sentence can give the student a deeper impression, and the euphonic rhythm can keep the boredom away. The following is a list of proverbs that can be used for pronunciation exercises. Sounds are marked with boldface instead of being represented by phonetic symbols because the American and British symbols are different, and some symbols may be distorted on the internet. - A friend in need is a friend indeed. - Every bullet has its billet. - A good wife and health is a man's best wealth. / East and west, home is best. - A drowning man will catch at a straw. - He laughs best who laughs last. - He who has an art has everywhere a part. - A little pot is soon hot. / A spot is most seen on the finest cloth. - New lords, new laws. / Walls have ears. - One man beats the bush, another man catches the bird. - Well begun is half done. - Finders keepers, losers weepers. - Kind words are the music of the world. / The early bird catches the worm. - Haste makes waste. - Little strokes fell great oaks. / As you sow you shall mow. - Good advice is beyond price. / Might makes right. - An ounce of discretion is worth a pound of learning. - No joy without annoy. - Constant dripping wears away a stone. - Penny wise, pound foolish./ Practice makes perfect. - There is nothing which has been bitter before being ripe. - Time and tide wait for no man. - A bird in hand is worth two in the wood. / Every dog has his day. - Care killed the cat. - A good name is better than a golden girdle. - Fair feathers make fair fowls. / Birds of a feather flock together. - Even the weariest river winds somewhere safe to sea. - Something is better than nothing. / Birds of a feather flock together. - Least said, soonest mended. / More haste, less speed. - A lazy youth, a lousy age. - No sunshine but hath some shadow. / Better be sure than sorry. - Labor is often the father of leisure. - Work has bitter root but sweet fruit. - There is no royal road to learning. - It is hard to be high and humble. / Do on the hills as you would do in the hall. - Everybody has his merits and faults. - No garden without its weeds. - Do not count your chickens before they are hatched. - Every Jack has his Jill. - Try before you trust. - Better be drunk than drowned. - A miss is as good as a mile. / Many a little makes a mickle. - A stitch in time saves nine. - Seeing is believing. / Everything must have a beginning. - Look before you leap. / A cracked bell can never sound well. - Willful waste makes woeful want. / Where there is a will, there is a way. Although many societies still exist under a gender binary, more and more around the world are becoming more accepting of ideas of gender outside of strict male and female categories. An important thing to know about gender in this form is that the people who exist within them have always existed but have not always been free to express themselves. One example of this is people who are transgender, those who were labeled based on their biological sex, but eventually come to realize that label does not fit what they identify with. In addition to this, some people do not experience gender in a way that fits into a binary at all, and these people are called either non binary or genderqueer. Title: How to Calculate the Area of a Scalene Triangle A scalene triangle is a type of triangle where all three sides are different lengths and all interior angles are different as well. To calculate its area, you'll need to know one side length and the corresponding height (the perpendicular distance from that side to the opposite vertex). Here's a step-by-step guide on calculating the area of a scalene triangle with clear explanations for each step. ### Step 1: Understand the Basics The formula for finding the area of any triangle, including a scalene triangle, is given by: Area = (base * height) / 2 where 'base' refers to any side of the triangle, and 'height' denotes the perpendicular distance between the base and the opposite vertex. This concept relies on Heron's Formula or trigonometry if only side lengths are known without the height. ### Step 2: Identify the Known Side and Height Look at your scalene triangle and identify which side has been specified as the base. Also, locate or determine the height relative to this base. Remember, the height is always perpendicular to the chosen base. If it isn't provided directly, you may have to compute it using geometric principles. ### Step 3: Measure the Base and Height Using a ruler or measuring tool, accurately measure the length of the base and the height. Record these values carefully since they will be used in your calculation. ### Step 4: Plug Values into the Formula Substitute the measured base and height values into the area formula: Area = (base * height) / 2 For example, if the base measures 5 units and the height measures 3 units, then: Area = (5 * 3) / 2 ### Step 5: Perform the Calculation Multiply the base times the height first, following the order of operations (BIDMAS/BODMAS), then divide by two: Area = (15) / 2 Area = 7.5 square units So, the calculated area of the scalene triangle is 7.5 square units. Key Tips and Guidelines: 1. Always double-check your measurements before performing calculations; even small errors can significantly affect results. 2. Make sure your measurement unit remains consistent throughout the process. 3. When dealing with more complex situations where the height isn't readily available, consider using sine functions (if angle measures are known) or Heron's Formula (when all side lengths are known but heights aren't). 4. Practice regularly to improve proficiency in such geometrical computations. On Thursday, he ate through four strawberries. Title: How to Get Rid of Love Handles: A Comprehensive Guide ========================================================= Love handles, those pesky pockets of fat that accumulate around the waistline, can be frustrating to deal with. However, by combining a healthy diet, targeted exercises, and good lifestyle habits, you can reduce their appearance and improve your overall fitness level. This extensive guide will walk you through each step needed to get rid of love handles effectively. ### Step 1: Understand the Causes of Love Handles Before diving into methods for reducing love handles, it's essential to understand what causes them. Love handles are primarily caused by excess body fat due to consuming more calories than burned off through daily activities or exercise. Genetics, stress, hormonal imbalances, and lack of sleep may also contribute to developing love handles. ### Step 2: Adopt a Balanced Diet Toronto is full of places to visit, eat, shop, and enjoy. In this Native American legend, Lizard sings a happy song about his home: "Zole, zole, zole, zole, zole, zole, rock is my home." Bear wants to learn the song. Lizard shares the song, but Bear keeps forgetting it. Bear insists again and again that Lizard re-teach her the song. Finally Lizard tells Bear that the reason she can't remember the song is that "rock" is not her home, "den" is her home. Bear goes off singing the adapted song, which she doesn't forget. For the tune to Lizard's song, you may use "Mary Had a Little Lamb." Change the last line from "rock is my home" to "den is my home" for Bear's song. Before you begin reading or telling the story, invite the group to join in when you sing: "Zole, zole, zole, rock (den) is my home." Note: Zole is pronounced "zoh/lee." There was a lizard who lived on a rock. He was very happy living on his rock. All day long Lizard would sing "Zole, zole, zole, rock is my home. Zole, zole, zole, rock is my home." One day a bear saw him sitting on the rock and heard him singing. She wondered why the lizard was so happy. So she asked him, "Why are you so happy?" Lizard said that he was happy because he was at home and the rock was a great place to live. "Oh," said Bear. She went back to her home and thought "I wish I knew that song, so I would be as happy as the lizard." The next day, Bear went back to Lizard's rock. She asked Lizard to teach her the song. Lizard said he would be very happy to teach it to her. He sang "Zole, zole, zole, rock is my home, zole, zole, zole, rock is my home." That made Bear feel good again. She went home. But on the way, she forgot the song. The next day, she went back to Lizard and asked him if he would teach her the song again. Lizard said he would be very happy to teach the song again. He sang "Zole, zole, zole, rock is my home, zole, zole, zole, rock is my home." Again Bear went home, and again she forgot the song. So she went back to Lizard one more time. This time, Lizard was asleep on his warm rock in the sunshine. What could Bear do? She decided to pick Lizard up and put him in a bag. Then she could take Lizard to her home. After his nap, he could teach Bear his song. On the way, Lizard woke up. He did not know where he was. He knew he wasn't at home, on his rock. He was very scared. He didn't know what to do. He decided to sing his song so he wouldn't be so frightened. He sang "Zole, zole, zole, rock is my home, zole, zole, zole, rock is my home." When Bear got home, she opened the bag. "Where are we?" asked Lizard. "This is my home," said Bear. "I'm sorry if I scared you when I brought you here." Now Lizard saw that Bear didn't live on a rock. Now he understood why Bear couldn't learn the song. He told Bear that she couldn't learn the song because she didn't live on a rock. Bear was disappointed. "But," Lizard said, "I can make you a song that you can learn and remember." He sang "Zole, zole, zole, den is my home, zole, zole, zole, den is my home." Bear learned that song very easily and she was very happy. This work is made possible by the generosity of individual donors and congregations. Please consider making a donation today. It's almost gone it looks almost like a star now. [puts the prize down on the table] Hello young scientists! Today we're going to talk about stars, galaxies, and how astronomers study their movements. It's like watching a beautiful dance of celestial bodies! Imagine you have a special telescope that can take pictures of starlight, creating something called a "spectrum." A spectrum is like a rainbow of light, where each color tells us something unique about the star. Now, when we look at not just one star, but a whole group of stars together in a galaxy, things get even more interesting! To understand these galactic dances, we need to measure how fast the stars are moving. One way to do this is by comparing the starlight spectra we mentioned earlier. Let's say we pick a common kind of star found in elliptical galaxies, called a K2III star. We then remove its "background noise," or continuous light, and rearrange the remaining starlight into equal spaced steps - sort of like organizing your toys on a shelf! This process is called "rebinding" and helps us analyze the data better. Now comes the fun part! To create our artificial galaxy spectrum, let's imagine taking our favorite K2III star and playing with its speed. By making it move faster and slower, we spread out its colors, similar to how spreading out a deck of cards changes the pattern. In our code, we use something called a Gaussian Losvd (Line Of Sight Velocity Distribution) to represent all those mixed up star speeds. The problem our friend encountered was that after combining the K2III star's spectrum with the Gaussian Losvd, the brightness of the combined spectrum became very small. Why did this happen? Well, think about blending paint colors; sometimes mixing two bright paints results in a duller shade than expected. The same thing happens here - multiplying the star's spectrum with the Gaussian Losvd reduces the overall brightness. But don't worry, there are tricks to bring back the brightness while still understanding the starry dance! So remember, studying the universe takes practice and patience, just like putting together a big puzzle. And who knows, maybe someday you could discover a new cosmic dance or learn secrets hidden within those twinkling lights! The climate of the Arctic region has varied significantly in the past. As recently as 55 million years ago, during the eocene epoch, the region reached an average annual temperature of 10-20 C (50-68 F). Title: How to Block Candy Crush (Or Any Other App) on Facebook Introduction: In this tutorial, we will guide you through the process of blocking an app, such as Candy Crush, on Facebook. This can be useful if you're tired of seeing constant game requests from friends or simply want to declutter your news feed. We will provide clear, step-by-step instructions along with explanations and helpful tips to ensure that you can successfully block any unwanted apps on Facebook. Step 1: Log into Your Facebook Account Visit <https://www.facebook.com/> and enter your email address or phone number and password to log in. It is essential to access your account before proceeding because only account holders have the authority to manage their privacy settings and applications connected to their profile. Key tip: If you are using a public computer or sharing your device with others, remember to sign out after completing these steps to protect your personal information. Step 2: Navigate to Settings & Privacy Once logged in, click on the arrow located at the top right corner of your screen next to the question mark icon. A dropdown menu will appear; select 'Settings & Privacy,' then choose 'Settings.' You will now see various categories related to managing your account, including Security, Privacy, Timeline and Tagging, and Blocking. Guidelines: Make sure you are familiar with other sections within the Settings page, especially those pertaining to security and privacy, as they allow you to customize your overall experience further. Step 3: Access the Blocking Section From the left sidebar, find and click on the 'Blocking' option under the Privacy section. The Block Users page will load, displaying options to block users and apps. Keep in mind that no one will receive notification when you block them unless they attempt to interact with your content directly. Step 4: Locate the App to Be Blocked Scroll down until you reach the 'Block apps' section. Here, type the name of the app you wish to block - in our example, Candy Crush - into the text box labeled 'Block apps by name.' As you begin typing, Facebook will generate suggestions based on your input. Select the correct app once it appears in the list. Step 5: Confirm the Block Action After entering the app's name correctly, click the blue 'Block' button to the right of the text box. A confirmation window will pop up, asking if you truly want to prevent this app from interacting with your timeline posts and requesting access to your data. Click 'Confirm' to finalize the action. Congratulations! At this point, you have successfully blocked the selected app on Facebook. From here onward, the chosen application should cease sending you invitations and updates. Additional Tips: * To unblock an app later, return to the same Blocking section in your Facebook Settings. Find the 'Block apps' area again, locate the app in question within the list below the text box, and click the corresponding 'Unblock' button beside its name. Confirm your choice in the subsequent window. * Remember that blocking individual games may not halt all notifications from specific gaming platforms like King or Zynga since some might operate multiple titles. Instead, consider adjusting broader notification settings associated with those developers. * Regularly review your active app permissions via the 'Apps and Websites' section found inside the Settings menu. Remove unnecessary or unused connections to maintain control over your data and reduce cluttered notifications. There he has a band-aid on him. Remember the little brown animal that eats nuts? The measure of how much heat is needed to cause a change in temperature for a material is the specific heat capacity of the material. If the particles in the material are hard to move, then more energy is needed to make them move quickly, so a lot of heat will cause a small change in temperature. A different particle that is easier to move will need less heat for the same change in temperature. "A pleasant day, Georges, somewhat fatiguing, but not without interest," he said. On Tuesday, he ate through two pears. Title: How to Change a Theme in Gmail Introduction: Gmail is an email service developed by Google. It offers various customization options, including themes that allow you to personalize your inbox's appearance. This tutorial will guide you through changing a theme in Gmail, with clear, detailed steps and helpful tips along the way. Step 1: Sign in to your Gmail account Visit the Gmail website (<https://www.gmail.com>) and enter your email address and password to sign in. Having access to your account ensures you can modify its settings according to your preferences. Step 2: Access your Gmail Settings After signing in, click on the gear icon located at the upper-right corner of your Gmail interface. A dropdown menu will appear; select 'See all settings' from this list. Taking you to the settings page enables you to configure display density, labels, filters, and other elements, like the theme. Step 3: Navigate to the Themes tab From the settings page, locate the tabs near the top of the screen - General, Labels, Accounts and Import, Filters and Blocked Addresses, Forwarding and POP/IMAP, Offline, and Chat. Click on the 'Themes' tab. Going to the designated section allows you to choose and apply different visual styles to your Gmail inbox effortlessly. Step 4: Explore available themes You will now see two categories of themes - 'Light' and 'Dark'. Under these categories are predefined background images or solid colors that you can set as your new theme. To preview a specific theme, hover over the thumbnail image. You may also click on the thumbnail if you want to view the entire theme in detail before applying it. Familiarizing yourself with the choices lets you pick a design that suits your taste best. Key Tips and Guidelines: If you wish to maintain high contrast between text and background regardless of the chosen theme, ensure that the 'Default' option under the 'Accessibility' heading is selected. Choosing light-colored or simple themes improves readability, particularly when reading emails in bright environments. Step 5: Apply your preferred theme Once you have identified your ideal theme, click on its corresponding thumbnail to apply it immediately. Applying the desired theme changes the look of your inbox instantly, enhancing user experience. Step 6: Save Changes After selecting a new theme, scroll down to the bottom of the page and click 'Save Changes.' Saving modifies the current configuration based on your choice. Failure to save might result in losing unsaved alterations due to session timeout or navigating away from the page. Conclusion: Changing a theme in Gmail provides a fresh perspective while using the platform, potentially improving productivity and enjoyment. By following this comprehensive tutorial, users should feel confident in their ability to navigate through the process seamlessly. Remember always to explore available options thoroughly before making final decisions, ensuring satisfaction with the end results. Once upon a time there was a very sick patient. Title: How to Change Your iTunes Payment Method on PC or Mac Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through changing your payment method on iTunes when using a PC or Mac. This is useful if your current payment method is no longer valid, or if you want to switch to a different card or payment option. Follow these steps carefully to ensure that your new payment information is correctly updated in your iTunes account. Key Tips & Guidelines: 1. Make sure you have access to an active and accepted payment method (credit/debit card, PayPal, etc.) before proceeding with this tutorial. 2. Update your Apple ID settings only from a trusted device or network to prevent unauthorized changes to your account. 3. Keep your Apple ID password handy during the process. You may be asked to enter it for verification purposes. 4. If you encounter any issues while following this tutorial, contact Apple Support for assistance. Step 1: Open iTunes on your computer Launch iTunes on either your PC or Mac by clicking on its icon. The application should open up displaying various options such as 'Music', 'Movies', and 'TV Shows'. Why do this? By opening iTunes, we can access our account settings and change the payment method associated with our Apple ID. Step 2: Sign in with your Apple ID Click on the Account menu located at the top left corner of the window and select 'Sign In' if you are not already logged in. Enter your Apple ID email address and password, then click 'Sign In'. Why sign in? To make changes to your account settings, including updating the payment method, you need to be signed in to your Apple ID. Step 3: Access Account Information Once again, go to the Account menu and choose 'Account Info'. A new window will appear prompting you to re-enter your Apple ID password for security reasons; type it in and press 'Enter' or click 'Sign In'. Why view your account info? Here, you can see all details related to your Apple ID, including the currently linked payment method. Step 4: Manage Payments On the Account Information page, scroll down until you find the 'Payment Information' section. Click on 'Edit'. Now, update your preferred payment method by selecting one from the dropdown list or adding a new one. Don't forget to fill out any necessary fields like billing addresses for credit cards. Why edit payments? Updating your payment information here ensures that future purchases made via iTunes will use the correct funding source. Step 5: Save Changes After entering the required details for your new payment method, click on the 'Done' button at the bottom right corner of the screen. Confirm the successful update by checking whether the newly added payment method appears under 'Payment Information'. Why save changes? Saving confirms and applies the updates to your Apple ID. Without saving, your modifications won't take effect. Conclusion: Congratulations! You've now successfully changed your iTunes payment method on your PC or Mac. Remember to always keep your account details secure and up-to-date. Should you face any difficulties, reach out to Apple Support for further guidance. Trains could not blast through the drifts on the tracks and had to be dug out. Coal supplies ran low. Schools were closed. Families could afford to heat only a few rooms in their homes. Drought dries out the soil As the spring of 1936 approached, Iowans hoped that all the snow had been a good sign. Surely the drought of the last few summers was over. As the weather warmed up, farmers searched the sky for signs of rain. "There would be black clouds, just as black as black, and lightning and thunder. The clouds would reach clear to the ground and they'd come rolling in," said J. Bruce Haddock. But the clouds seemed to tease the farmers-rain fell, but "only a few drops." As the drought continued, the corn crop withered in the fields. "On the Fourth of July," Haddock remembered, "the corn was just as tall as the wheel on the cultivator. And by a month later it was as though someone had pulled it back into the ground." The Dust Bowl blows into Iowa States farther west were suffering from the drought too. As plants died on the Great Plains, there was nothing to hold the soil in place. Winds picked up the soil and carried it in dark, swirling clouds of dust. These states were called the Dust Bowl. But the clouds of dust did not stop at state boundaries. They hit Iowa too. "The dust settled so thickly on the pastures that the cattle would not eat," author James Hearst wrote later about 1934-1936, "and cows, and calves, and steers wandered about bawling their hunger. We found it hard to believe. We all knew about dust storms in the dry plains of the Southwest, but for drought and wind and dust to sweep, like a plague, over the fertile fields of Blackhawk County, Iowa, seemed a bad dream." Dust drifted two or three feet high, around fences and buildings. Dust sifted into houses, under doors and through cracks around windows. It filled the air, darkening the day. "The year I came here to teach," recalled Georgette Haddock, "that fall the dust storms were here. And most of the time for several weeks that fall we'd have our lights on, because it was like evening." Grasshoppers thrived in the dry, hot weather. They attacked the few crops still growing. Ruby Howorth recalled how grasshoppers on their Crawford County farm seemed to devour everything. "We left a pitchfork sit outside. You could see where they chewed into the wood on the pitchfork. Now that sounds crazy, but that's the truth." Who walks tall tail between the bottles? You throw such a fit and then want your ears. Global warming is mostly because of people burning things, like gasoline to make cars go and natural gas to keep houses warm. But the heat from the burning itself only makes the world a tiny bit warmer: it is the carbon dioxide from the burning which is the biggest part of the problem. Among the greenhouse gases, the increase of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere is the main cause of global warming, as predicted by Svante Arrhenius a hundred years ago, confirming the work of Joseph Fourier more than 200 years ago. When people burn fossil fuels like coal, oil and natural gas this adds carbon dioxide into the air. This is because fossil fuels contain lots of carbon and burning means joining most of the atoms in the fuel with oxygen. When people cut down many trees (deforestation), this means less carbon dioxide is taken out of the atmosphere by those plants. Once there was a little girl who was very kind. She was not aware that some people who were passing by had noticed what she had done and quietly admired her for her kindness. The little girl had learned an important lesson that day: it is important to always be kind to those around you and care for those in need.
